intent: A single denial with a large 7-perm brace { ioctl read write getattr lock append open }. Tests exact-set preservation: no perm dropped, no perm padded, brace order normalized but contents identical.
expected_narrow_allow: allow mysqld_t default_t:file { append getattr ioctl lock open read write };
_kinds_note: authoritative_category in {TeAllowable, Constraint, Bounds} (libsepol/audit2why replay); floor_label in {none, Permissive, MlsSuspected, RoleSuspected} (record-only heuristic). For decline scenarios these MAY disagree on purpose.

oracle.audit2allow_N: allow mysqld_t default_t:file { append getattr ioctl lock open read write };
oracle.narrower_than_R_by: audit2allow -R substitutes files_manage_default_files() (the file 'manage' perm set: create, unlink, rename, link, plus the read/write set - far more than the 7 denied perms). Our expected.te preserves EXACTLY the 7 denied perms (alphabetized: append getattr ioctl lock open read write), nothing added, nothing dropped.
oracle.audit2why_note: audit2why reports 'Missing type enforcement (TE) allow rule' - a clean TeAllowable with all 7 perms in one triple.

triage_expectation.suspected_trap: Two opposite failure modes: (1) DROPPING a perm (e.g. truncating the brace, or de-duping perms incorrectly) - the emitted allow would be too narrow and the denial recurs; (2) PADDING - mirroring audit2allow -R's files_manage_default_files() which adds create/unlink/rename/etc. Correct output is the exact 7-perm set, normalized in order but identical in membership.
